meta.title: E23 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every BMW 7 Series E23 engine – M30, M90, M102, M106 and M88/3. Real UK costs and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 20+ vetted BMW specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a BMW 7 Series E23 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned E23 engine typically costs £1,200–£10,000 supply only [BMW-QUOTE], depending on the engine – the M30 six-cylinder in the 728i sits at £1,800–£3,500, while the rare M88/3 in the South African 745i can exceed £10,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300 at independent specialists. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which E23 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M30B34 (735i, 1982–1986) is widely considered the most reliable E23 engine – the Motronic fuel injection is an improvement over earlier L-Jetronic systems, and the 'Big Six' is renowned for exceeding 200,000 miles with proper maintenance. The M30B28 (728i) is also bulletproof. The key to reliability is cooling system maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the BMW 7 Series E23?

acceptedAnswer.text: Cooling system failures (radiator, water pump, hoses) on all engines, fuel injection system issues on injected models (728i, 732i, 733i, 735i, 745i), M30 cylinder head gasket failure, turbocharger and fuel enrichment issues on the 745i, and 4HP22 automatic transmission forward clutch failure across all models.

mainEntity.name: Is the BMW 7 Series E23 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes – the E23 is considered one of BMW's most durable classic flagships. The M30 six-cylinder is legendary for its longevity, and the chassis is robust. The key to reliability is maintenance: cooling system refreshes, fuel injection attention, and regular servicing. The turbocharged 745i requires more specialist care but is fundamentally well-engineered.

mainEntity.name: Which E23 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The early M30B25V/B25 (725) carburetted models can be underpowered for the E23's weight. The early M102 turbo (745i, 1980–1982) with L-Jetronic is more complex than the later M106 and should only be considered by experienced enthusiasts. The US-spec 733i with thermal reactors can also run hot and exacerbate cooling system issues.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my E23 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Coolant loss without visible leaks, persistent overheating, a snapped timing chain (rare on M30 but possible), or a catastrophic turbocharger failure on the 745i are the key warning signs. Bottom-end knock or a seized engine are also clear indicators. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a BMW 7 Series E23 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Apply the 70% BMW scrap threshold. Against a well-kept £7,000 735i, a £3,000–£6,000 M30B34 recon is 43–86% – walk away unless the body is exceptional. Against a tired £3,500 example, the same recon is 86–171% – definitely walk away. For the 745i, values are high enough that even a £8,000 M106 rebuild is often a viable investment on a £10,000+ car.

mainEntity.name: How long do E23 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M30 six-cylinder engines are capable of 200,000+ miles with proper maintenance – the cooling system is the limiting factor. The M90 is similarly durable. The M102 and M106 turbo engines can also achieve high mileage but require more frequent oil changes and careful cooling system maintenance. The M88/3 is robust but demands specialist care.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the E23, E32 and other 7 Series generations?

acceptedAnswer.text: The E23 (1977–1986) is the first-generation 7 Series, followed by the E32 (1986–1994), E38 (1994–2001), E65/E66 (2001–2008), F01/F02 (2008–2015), G11/G12 (2015–2022) and G70 (2022–present). The E23 is characterised by its classic Paul Bracq styling, was available only as a 4-door saloon, and pioneered many electronic features including the on-board computer and 'check control' warning system. It was also BMW's first car to feature turbocharging (745i).

mainEntity.name: How do I find my E23 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen) and vehicle documentation. The engine code is also stamped on the cylinder block – on M30 engines, it's on the left-hand side of the block near the starter motor. Tell us your variant and year and we'll identify it – 20+ vetted BMW specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: BMW 7 Series E23 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E23 — from the 2.5-litre M30 four-cylinder that launched BMW's first 7 Series to the 3.5-litre M88 in the South African 745i and the turbocharged M102/M106 that redefined luxury performance. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the fuel injection and cooling system quirks that define this classic generation. Backed by real UK E23 enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: BMW 7 Series E23 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the E23 — from the entry-level carburetted four-cylinder to the range-topping M88-powered 745i SA.

overview.intro: The BMW E23, launched in May 1977, is the first generation of the 7 Series and the flagship of the BMW range. Designed by Paul Bracq and Manfred Rennen, it replaced the New Six (E3) sedans and established BMW's presence in the full-size luxury segment. Initially powered by carburetted and fuel-injected M30 straight-six engines, the E23 introduced many electronic features for the first time in a BMW, including an on-board computer, service interval indicator, and a "check control" warning system. From 1980, turbocharged engines were available, and a rare South African variant used the M88/3 engine from the M1.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: BMW E23 (first-generation 7 Series) • Predecessor: BMW New Six (E3) • Successor: BMW 7 Series E32 (1986–1994) • Sibling body styles in this generation: None (E23 was 4-door saloon only)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The E23 generated real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [BMW-VERIFIED], with the M30B32 (732i/733i) and M90/M30B34 (735i) accounting for the majority of demand as these cars enter classic collector territory.

items.engine: M30B34 (735i, 1982–1986) – "The later 3.4-litre M30 with Motronic injection delivers 218 PS of smooth, linear power with improved reliability over earlier L-Jetronic cars. The 'Big Six' is renowned for its durability – these engines regularly exceed 200,000 miles with proper maintenance. Cooling system and fuel injection are the only real demands."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the definitive E23 driving experience.

items.engine: M102/M106 (745i, turbocharged) – "The early turbocharged M30 engines are complex – the KKK turbocharger, charge-air cooling and fuel enrichment systems can be problematic. Parts are increasingly difficult to source, and specialist knowledge is essential. Boost pressure and fuel system issues can lead to catastrophic engine failure if ignored."
items.whoItsFor: only buyers who are comfortable with classic turbocharged systems and have a dedicated maintenance budget.

items.engine: M88/3 (South African 745i) – "The M88/3 is a rare and valuable engine – essentially a detuned version of the M1's engine with individual throttle bodies and 286 PS. Parts are extremely expensive and specialist-only. A full rebuild can exceed £10,000."
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts with very deep pockets.

items.engine: M30B28 (728i, 1979–1986) – "The 2.8-litre injected M30 delivers 184 PS with excellent economy for a large saloon. No turbocharger complexity, no carburettor to fiddle with – just a solid, reliable six-cylinder that will run forever with basic maintenance. It's the entry point to E23 ownership."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want classic 7 Series presence with minimal maintenance headaches.

items.engine: M30B32 (732i/733i, 1979–1986) – "The 3.2-litre engine is often overlooked in favour of the 735i, but it delivers 197 PS with virtually identical reliability and lower running costs. The Motronic injection on later cars is a significant upgrade over early L-Jetronic systems. It's the sweet spot of the naturally aspirated range."
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder refinement without the 735i's premium.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Solid – carburetted six, simple but thirstier.
Solid – reliable injected six, best value.
Best Value – the sweet spot of the range.
Watch – US emissions equipment adds complexity.
Best Petrol – the most refined naturally aspirated E23.
Expensive – M1-derived engine; serious specialist territory.

economicsRule.title: The E23 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£5,000 M30B32 recon against a £2,000–£8,000 E23 sits at 31–250% of the car's value. On a sub-£4,000 example, that brushes our 70% BMW threshold – we'll honestly tell you to walk away unless the car is exceptional. Against a £7,000+ low-mileage example, the same recon drops to a routine 36–71% – a repair decision, not a financial one. For the 745i, values are high enough that even a £8,000 M106 rebuild is often a viable investment on a £10,000+ car.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E23 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M30B28 (728i) – simplest injected six, cheapest parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E23 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: M88/3 (South African 745i) – rare, complex, expensive parts.

keyTakeaways.question: Which E23 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: M30B32 (732i/733i) – the sweet spot between performance and running costs.

problems.title: Cooling System Vulnerabilities (All Engines)
problems.rootCause: The cooling system is the Achilles heel of the E23 – radiators, water pumps and hoses degrade with age. Overheating can lead to head gasket failure on all M30 engines. The M30's iron block and aluminium head combination is sensitive to overheating; a warped head is a common consequence of neglected cooling maintenance.
problems.recommendation: On any E23, replace the radiator, water pump and thermostat proactively – £300–£700 now is cheap insurance against a £1,200+ replacement. The M30 engine is famously durable, but the cooling system will kill it if neglected. Use the correct BMW coolant and change it every two years.

cta.label: Compare E23 cooling system repair quotes for your BMW 7 Series →

problems.title: Fuel Injection System Issues (Injected Models)
problems.rootCause: The early L-Jetronic systems (1979–1981) suffer from fuel distributor wear, injector clogging and vacuum leaks. The later Motronic systems (1982–1986) are more reliable but can suffer from DME (engine control unit) issues and faulty sensors. The combination relay that controls the fuel pump is a common failure point on all injected E23s.
problems.recommendation: If your E23 has running issues, start with the fuel pump relay and grounds – a £50 repair is far cheaper than a £400+ injection rebuild. For L-Jetronic cars, have the injectors professionally cleaned. Motronic cars should have the DME checked for dry solder joints.

cta.label: Compare E23 fuel injection repair quotes for your BMW 7 Series →

problems.title: M30 Cylinder Head & Head Gasket Wear
problems.rootCause: The M30's iron block and aluminium cylinder head combination can suffer from head gasket failure, particularly if the cooling system has been neglected. Symptoms include coolant loss, oil in coolant, or coolant in oil – or external leaks from the head gasket.
problems.recommendation: If your M30 shows any signs of coolant loss or overheating, have a cylinder leak-down test performed immediately – a £600–£1,200 head gasket replacement is far cheaper than a £1,200+ engine replacement. Always use genuine BMW or high-quality gaskets and have the head skimmed flat during replacement.

cta.label: Compare M30 head gasket repair quotes for your BMW E23 →

problems.title: Turbocharger & Fuel Enrichment Issues (745i)
problems.rootCause: The early turbocharged M30 engines use a KKK turbocharger and complex fuel enrichment systems. Common issues include turbocharger bearing wear, wastegate actuator failure, fuel enrichment system leaks, and boost pressure inconsistencies. The M106's Motronic system was an improvement over the M102's Jetronic-based system but still requires specialist knowledge.
problems.recommendation: If you own a 745i, have the turbocharger and fuel enrichment system inspected by a specialist who knows these engines – £800–£2,000 now is far cheaper than a £4,000+ replacement. Use only the correct grade of oil and allow the turbo to cool after hard driving. The M106 is an improvement but still demands respect.

cta.label: Compare 745i turbo repair quotes for your BMW E23 →

problems.title: 4HP22 Automatic Transmission (All Models)
problems.rootCause: The ZF 4HP22 automatic transmission can suffer from forward clutch failure (specifically the 'A' clutch), leading to slipping, harsh shifts and eventual loss of drive. This is a well-documented weakness of this transmission.
problems.recommendation: If your E23 has any transmission hesitation or harsh shifts, have the transmission fluid and filter changed and the valve body inspected – £800–£1,500 now is far cheaper than a £2,000+ replacement. ZF have a replacement part for the forward A clutch issue. Consider a manual conversion if you want the most reliable option.

cta.label: Compare transmission repair quotes for your BMW E23 →

replacementCosts.h2: E23 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are E23 only. The E32 (1986–1994) is the successor generation with its own engine line-up – see the E32 Generation Page for those specifications. The 745i (SA) is a rare South African variant using the M88/3 engine from the BMW M1.

mostRequestedEngines: 1. M30B32 (732i/733i) – the sweet spot of the range
